name: The Terra Cotta Vessel
story: Born from rich ochre clay dug near the Colorado plateau, this vessel spent 18 hours in a wood-fire kiln before its botanical etchings emerged from the smoke.
description: Hand-thrown from rich ochre clay and fired at high temperature, this vessel features deeply etched botanical patterns inspired by ancient Southwestern motifs. A bold statement piece for any curated interior.

name: Obscura Angular Vase
story: Studio ceramicist Lena Voss threw this vase at midnight — the silence, she says, shapes the tension you feel in its sharp geometric shoulders.
description: Crafted in matte black stoneware with sharp geometric shoulders, this vase channels the quiet tension of modern sculpture. Its dark, light-absorbing surface creates a striking contrast against pale walls.

name: Azure Fracture Bottle
story: Glazed twice, dipped in cold seawater, then fired again — every turquoise fracture in its surface maps a real tide pool from the Oregon coast.
description: Glazed in layers of turquoise and teal, this squat bottle evokes sea-worn rock pools and tidal textures.

name: Ribbed Alabaster Pair
story: Slip-cast in the same mold two mornings apart, each vessel carries a faint memory of its sibling.
description: These creamy white vessels are slip-cast with rhythmic vertical ribbing that catches ambient light beautifully.

name: Charred Kintsugi Form
finish: Dark Glaze with Gold Slip Repair
story: Cracked intentionally at leather-hard stage, then mended with gold-tinted slip.
description: Inspired by the Japanese philosophy of finding beauty in imperfection.

name: Rose Bloom Vessel
story: Painted stripe by stripe in a garden studio in Lisbon.
description: Striped in soft blush, lavender, and mint, this tall vase is wheel-thrown and hand-painted.

name: Sagebrush Tall Pitcher
story: Dried for three weeks on an open New Mexico windowsill.
description: Thrown on a slow wheel and dried under desert sun, this tall pitcher carries earthy sage-green tones.

name: Ivory Moon Jar
story: Two half-spheres pressed together by hand.
description: Modeled after the iconic Joseon-era moon jars.

name: Copper Drip Flask
story: The copper oxide glaze was mixed with local iron-rich river clay.
description: Iron-rich clay meets a flowing copper-oxide glaze in this dramatic flask form.

name: Slate Pinch Bowl
story: No wheel, no mold — just thumbs and patience.
description: Built entirely by hand without a wheel, this small bowl bears the honest fingerprint impressions of its maker.

name: Celadon Footed Cup
story: A single carved line circles the body before firing.
description: Inspired by classical Chinese celadon ware, this footed cup shimmers in a pale jade glaze.

name: Obsidian Raku Bowl
story: Pulled from the kiln at 900°C and plunged into newspaper.
description: Pulled from the kiln at peak temperature and smoked in a steel drum.

name: Blush Coil Vase
story: Each coil was rolled by hand on a linen cloth.
description: Built using the ancient coiling technique, this vase retains the subtle ridges of its construction.

name: Fossil Imprint Plate
story: Real Oregon fern fronds were pressed into the soft clay before sunrise.
description: Real botanical specimens are pressed into soft clay before firing.

name: Indigo Slab Vase
story: Rolled from a single slab of white stoneware, then folded into sharp planes.
description: Hand-built from rolled slabs of white stoneware and dipped in a deep cobalt-indigo glaze.

name: Terracotta Flower Brick
story: Modelled on Victorian florist bricks found in a Bristol antique market.
description: Modeled after antique florist bricks, this low rectangular vessel has five cylindrical holes.

name: White Porcelain Bud Vase
story: Thrown so thin you can read newspaper through the walls.
description: Thrown from translucent porcelain on an electric wheel and left unadorned.

name: Hammered Bronze Pot
story: Three layers of iron oxide were brushed on and scraped back before firing.
description: A wood-fired pot whose exterior is brushed with layers of iron and manganese oxides.

name: Lavender Haze Bottle
story: Glazed under a fog machine in a highland studio.
description: A graceful bottle with a narrow neck and swollen body, glazed in shifting lavender and grey tones.

name: Cracked Earth Planter
story: The crawling glaze was mixed with coarse desert sand.
description: The surface of this cylindrical planter is deliberately textured to resemble sun-cracked desert earth.

name: Sea Foam Yunomi
story: Glazed in frothy aquamarine, this yunomi was inspired by a dawn walk on a black-sand beach.
description: A Japanese-style yunomi tea cup glazed in frothy aquamarine and white.

name: Midnight Teapot
story: Months in the making — the bamboo handle alone took six attempts.
description: Wheel-thrown in dark iron-bearing clay and reduction fired, this teapot achieves a near-black surface.

name: Sand Dune Sculpture
story: The maker spent a week in the Sahara before building this.
description: An abstract hand-built sculpture that mimics the rippled undulations of a sand dune.

name: Forest Green Amphora
finish: Deep Forest Green with Wood-Ash Drips
story: Wood-ash from a studio fire dripped down the shoulders during the final kiln firing.
description: Inspired by ancient Mediterranean storage vessels, this two-handled amphora is glazed in a deep forest green.

name: Ash White Lantern
story: Each aperture was cut by hand with a needle tool at 2am.
description: Pierced with delicate geometric apertures and glazed in natural wood-ash white.

name: Russet Harvest Jug
story: Filled with cider on the night it was finished.
description: A wide-bodied jug with a generous handle and pouring lip, glazed in warm russet and amber tones.

name: Pebble Stack Trio
story: Three river pebbles carried home in a jacket pocket became the templates.
description: Three hand-formed vessels of graduating size, each smoothed to resemble a river pebble.

name: Emerald Faceted Bottle
story: Cut with a wire tool while still wet, each facet catches light differently as the day moves.
description: Each face of this multifaceted bottle is carefully cut from thrown clay before firing.
